name: Meathead's Last-Meal Ribs
description: Meathead Goldwyn's famous Last-Meal Ribs (AmazingRibs.com) — dry-brined, coated in Memphis Dust, and cooked low-and-slow at 225°F until they pass the bend test. No smoker required: a two-zone gas grill with a foil pouch of wood chips does the job. Sized for ~2 racks (6 people).
recipeIngredient: 2 racks baby back ribs (or St. Louis-cut spare ribs)
Kosher salt (about 1/4 tsp per pound of meat — ribs are ~50% bone — applied separately up to 12 hours ahead)
Memphis Dust rub, about 2 tablespoons per side / ~4 tablespoons per rack (recipe below)
Kansas City-style BBQ sauce, optional, for serving
About 8 oz wood chips (hickory, apple, or cherry) for smoke
MEMPHIS DUST (makes a big reusable batch):
recipeInstructions: Prep the ribs: flip bone-side up and peel off the tough membrane (grip with a paper towel and pull). Trim any loose flap.

Dry-brine: because ribs are about 50% bone, use roughly 1/4 teaspoon kosher salt per pound of meat (half the usual 1/2 tsp/lb rule), sprinkled on up to 12 hours ahead. Salt goes on separately from the rub.

Make the Memphis Dust: stir all rub ingredients together, breaking up any sugar lumps. Optionally coat the meat with a thin layer of mustard (or just dampen it) as glue, then sprinkle on the Memphis Dust — about 2 tablespoons per side (~4 tablespoons per rack). No resting needed.

Set up a gas grill for two-zone indirect cooking: light only one burner (or one side). Place the ribs on the UNLIT side, meaty side up, and close the lid. Adjust the lit burner to hold about 225°F (don't drop below 200°F).

Add smoke: put about 4 oz wood chips in a holed foil pouch (or a smoke box) over the lit burner. When the smoke fades after 20-30 minutes, add another 4 oz. After the first hour (two loads), stop adding wood.
Cook at 225°F with the lid closed: baby backs about 3-4 hours, St. Louis/spares about 5-6 hours. Don't wrap and don't mop.
Check doneness with the bend test: lift the rack with tongs - when it bends and the surface cracks and the meat has pulled back from the bone tips, it's done (roughly 195-203°F).

Optional sauce/set: to firm up the bark or set sauce at the end, crank all the burners up (or slide the ribs over the direct-heat side) for a few minutes. If saucing, brush KC-style BBQ sauce on (about 6 tablespoons per rack) only during this last 15-30 minutes so the sugars set without burning, or serve sauce on the side.
Rest a few minutes, cut between the bones, and serve.
OVEN VARIATION (no grill): cook at 225°F on a rack over a foil-lined sheet pan, about 3-4 hours for baby backs; finish with sauce under the broiler for char. No real smoke indoors.
author: Meathead Goldwyn, AmazingRibs.com

ai.cliffNotes: Dry-brine, coat in Memphis Dust, and cook indirect at 225°F until the rack passes the bend test. A two-zone gas grill with a wood-chip pouch replaces a smoker; baby backs run 3-4 hours.
keyTips: No smoker needed: a two-zone gas grill IS your smoker - one burner lit, ribs on the cool side, lid down, held at ~225°F.

Meathead salts and rubs separately: dry-brine with kosher salt up to 12 hours ahead — but only ~1/4 tsp per pound for ribs (they're ~50% bone, so half the usual rate), then apply the salt-free Memphis Dust.
Peel the membrane off the bone side or the ribs turn chewy and the rub can't penetrate.
Only smoke during the first hour (two 4-oz loads). More wood makes them acrid and over-smoked; after that it's just gentle heat.
Cook to feel, not a number: the bend test (rack cracks when bent, meat pulls off the bones) beats a thermometer. Sauce, if any, goes on only at the very end so the sugar doesn't scorch.
